segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a title card for 'Oedipus the King, Part 1 of 2,' written by Sophocles and adapted by Robin Bond, presented by Western Norway University of Applied Sciences in Bergen 2019. As a low, sustained, and somewhat eerie drone sound begins, the scene transitions to a dimly lit theater stage bathed in red light, with a small audience seated on either side of a central aisle. On stage, several performers in period-style costumes are positioned near the back, including one holding a staff and others standing near props resembling trees or pillars. A woman in dark clothing walks down the aisle toward the stage, her silhouette stark against the red glow, while the drone continues, maintaining its ominous tone. She reaches the stage and turns, facing the group, as the drone intensifies slightly, becoming more prominent and slightly dissonant. Another performer in lighter clothing walks from the stage toward the audience, pausing mid-way. The lighting remains consistently red, creating a dramatic and somber atmosphere, now accompanied by distinct, rhythmic, almost percussive metallic clang sounds layered over the drone. Minimal movement from the background performers, who appear to be observing or waiting, continues as the metallic strikes become more frequent and intense, building in a crescendo. The stage setup includes a white chair on the left and a low platform, suggesting a minimalist, symbolic set design, all enveloped by the escalating, resonant soundscape.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in near darkness, illuminated only by red and blue stage lights, with silhouettes of performers moving across the stage, accompanied by a rhythmic, percussive sound, like a drum or large object being struck. As the lighting gradually brightens, the full stage becomes visible, featuring a black curtain backdrop, a white chair adorned with flowers on the left, and a low platform near center stage. A deep, resonant gong sound punctuates the atmosphere. A Performer in a long red robe, later identified as Oedipus, enters from the left, walking toward the center. A Performer with Staff, in dark, hooded, form-fitting clothing, stands motionless at the back right, holding a long staff. The Performer in Red approaches the Performer with Staff, gesturing with open hands as if in conversation or confrontation, and speaks with a commanding, theatrical male voice, asking, "My children, oh what is it that you want?" Meanwhile, on the right side of the stage, a group of Background Performers in period-style garments remain mostly stationary, observing. One of them, the Performer in Light Clothing, briefly interacts with the Woman in Dark Clothing by placing a hand on her shoulder before stepping back. The Woman in Dark Clothing, in a dark hooded robe, stands near the Performer with Staff, occasionally shifting her stance. The Performer with Staff, speaking with a slightly lower, more somber male voice, responds, "The city," elaborating that it is "filled alike with the smoke of sacrifice and cries of misery." The Performer in Red then identifies himself, stating, "My children, I have come myself. I, Oedipus." He then addresses the Performer with Staff, saying, "Now then, speak, old man, since you are the natural spokesman for these folk," and asks if they have gathered "in dread or in need," offering his help. The Performer with Staff replies, addressing him as "Lord Oedipus," and begins to speak of "requests." The White Chair remains untouched, and the Low Platform serves as the focal point of this dramatic, theatrical interaction, with lighting focused on the central figures.

He describes a city in distress, stating, "the city is at sea and can no longer lift her head above the fatal waves," and warns that "a deadly plague is upon the town," with "Hell's black halls... rich with cries of grief and pain." He addresses the Performer with Staff as "my Lord," thanking him for saving their lives from "cruel sins" and asserting that "a city is worth nothing without its citizens." Throughout this, the Performer with Staff, clad in dark hooded attire and holding a staff topped with a cross, stands mostly motionless, maintaining a solemn presence, occasionally shifting weight and gesturing subtly. To the right, the Woman in Dark Clothing stands near the Performer with Staff, her posture shifting slightly as she observes the exchange. The Performer in Light Clothing briefly places a hand on her shoulder before stepping back, suggesting a moment of comfort or connection. Nearby, the Background Performers remain mostly still, observing the central interaction, dressed in earth-toned, layered garments with head coverings. The White Chair on the left, adorned with a small bouquet, remains untouched. As the Performer in Red concludes his plea, the Performer with Staff responds with a deeper, authoritative voice, declaring, "I pity you, my children. I know well why you have come here. And what you want. I know..." The lighting remains consistent, focused on the central figures, while the audience silhouettes are visible in the foreground. The scene ends with a sudden dimming of lights, fading the stage into darkness.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens on a dimly lit stage, where a group of performers in period-style costumes are arranged. The Performer in Red, wearing a flowing red robe, walks from the left toward the center, approaching the Performer with Staff, who stands motionless on a low platform holding a cross-topped staff. The Performer in Red speaks with a dramatic, commanding male voice, declaring, "there is not one of you whose sickness matches mine," and then reveals his plan for safety, stating he has sent "my wife's very own brother, Creon." The camera zooms in on their interaction as the Performer with Staff, in dark hooded clothing, responds with a more deferential male voice, addressing the Performer in Red as "my lord" and confirming Creon's imminent arrival, adding, "His news are good, it seems." The Performer in Red expresses hope that Creon comes "with luck and saving grace," then calls out, "Creon, what news have you come to bring us?" A Woman in Dark Clothing observes nearby, briefly placing a hand on the Performer with Staff’s shoulder before stepping back, while Background Performers remain mostly still on the right. A new, distinct male voice, identified as Creon, replies, "Oh, the news are good, my lord. All our bad luck will be reversed and changed for the good, if things are done right," prompting the Performer in Red to question, "Done right?" The solemn, theatrical atmosphere is maintained by the focused lighting and slow, deliberate pace.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in near darkness, with a spotlight illuminating a Woman in Dark Clothing, clad in a textured, dark hooded robe, standing with her back to the audience, hands slightly raised in supplication. Simultaneously, a Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left, approaching with open, expressive hand gestures, engaging in a dramatic monologue. He speaks with a commanding, theatrical voice, declaring, "Freedom will only and only come if we with certainty find and identify the men who murdered the king. And kill them too. Or drive them, exile them away from this land." The two stand on a low platform, facing each other, with the Performer in Red occasionally stepping back and gesturing towards the Woman in Dark Clothing, whose blindfolded face is partially visible under her hood, maintaining a solemn posture. He continues, his tone shifting to a more pleading or questioning note, "Please, Oedipus, send me home. Is it, is it by far for you to bear your fate? For me to bear mine, if you obey." The lighting intensifies on the Woman in Dark Clothing, highlighting her robe's texture. The Performer in Red then moves to a white wooden chair adorned with flowers on the left, sits briefly, and rises again, gesturing towards her. He then becomes accusatory, stating, "You speak words out of keeping and unfriendly to the state which raised you by refusing to interpret your will. I see that what you say is far from fortunate. And I hold my peace to obviate the risk of suffering the same." The Woman in Dark Clothing continues her expressive hand gestures, sometimes touching her chest or raising them dramatically, while the Performer in Red responds with animated gestures, creating a ritualistic exchange. The camera occasionally zooms in on her, emphasizing her blindfolded state and emotional weight. The Performer in Red's voice rises to a forceful, almost shouting demand, "No! The scene concludes with both figures still engaged, the Performer in Red gesturing towards her, and the audience visible in silhouette in the foreground, some raising hands in response, indicating a live theatrical context.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, centered around a stone-arch prop and a white cross-back chair adorned with a small bouquet. The Woman in White Gown, dressed in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near the chair, engaging in a dramatic dialogue. She speaks with a calm, clear female voice, narrating, "He had a party of five. And an officer with them. Lies wrote in a single card." The Second Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, begins by adjusting his robe, then interjects with a strong, questioning male voice, "And who was it that told you this, my queen?" The Woman in White Gown responds, "A slave. Who escaped and came back home." The Second Performer in Red, gesturing expressively, presses, "This slave. Is he still in this house now?" She continues her narration, explaining, "Why no? When he came back, and saw you with power, and Lies being dead, he came to me. He begged me to send him to the fields as he would be rather herding sheep than being teased. So I sent him there." He bows his head in a moment of emotional weight before extending his hand toward her, asking urgently, "Well, how can we get him back?" She responds simply, "By Tinsley." He questions, "But why would he be doing that?" then quickly shifts, "I, I fear I have said too much. I wish to see him now, my wife!" The Woman in White Gown responds with subtle hand movements and shifts her stance, reciprocating his gesture by reaching out to him, reassuring him, "He will come, my lord." She begins to speak again, "But I am...", as the segment ends. The stage floor is decorated with colorful, crumpled paper resembling flowers, and in the foreground, silhouettes of the Audience are visible, some turning their heads or raising hands, with a hand holding a phone on a stick briefly entering from the left to capture the scene.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in near-total darkness, with only faint blue stage lights visible above, creating a mysterious and somber atmosphere. Simultaneously, a deep, resonant male voice begins a dramatic monologue, declaring, "furious, mad with rage," and describing how "their driver had attempted to push me" off the road, punctuated by a forceful thud. Gradually, a dim greenish light reveals a stage with several performers in period-style costumes. The Woman in Dark Clothing stands center stage with her back to the audience, while the Performer in Red and the Performer with Staff are positioned nearby. Background Performers stand near stylized tree and house props on the right, observing. The male voice continues, recounting a violent encounter: "When the old man saw this, he swung at my head with his walking stick," followed by another sharp thud, and his own retaliatory strike, "I swung at him with a stick of my own, my spear! Right to the ground he went!" The Woman in Dark Clothing turns and gestures expressively, interacting with the Performer in Red, who responds with subtle movements. The lighting shifts to a deep red, intensifying the emotional tone as the Woman in Dark Clothing raises her arms in a ritualistic pose, flanked by two other performers who mirror her gesture. The monologue culminates in a chilling declaration, "And I killed them all! I killed them all!" The Performer in Red then moves toward the left side of the stage, where the Second Performer in Red, seated on the White Chair beside the Stone-arch Prop, engages in a dramatic dialogue with the Woman in White Gown. She gestures with her hands, and he, the speaker of the ongoing monologue, responds with bowed head and outstretched hand, creating a moment of connection. The lighting brightens slightly, revealing more details: the Woman in White Gown wears a red headband and flowing gown with red trim, while the Second Performer in Red wears a long red robe over a white tunic. Colorful Fabric Props frame the stage, adding visual contrast. The Audience remains visible in silhouette, some turning their heads or raising hands, while a Hand with Phone on Stick enters from the left, capturing the scene. The Second Performer in Red's monologue continues, pondering a connection: "So yes, if there is a connection between this old man and King Laius..." and concluding with profound regret, "then there is no one more sorry than me." The interaction between the Second Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown culminates in a tender moment as they reach toward each other, their hands nearly touching, before the Second Performer in Red turns and walks away, leaving the Woman in White Gown alone by the Stone-arch Prop.

He continues, addressing her as "My wife," contemplating the grim possibilities of fleeing, never taking comfort, joining his mother in marriage, or killing his own father. His intense monologue persists as he states, "what little hope I have left rests with this man, this herdsman." The Second Performer in Red replies, "If his words are consistent with yours, I might then escape disaster." She presses further, "What was that you heard? That was so decisive?" to which he begins to answer, "You said, according to this man's story," before the minute concludes. The Woman in Dark Clothing stands to the right, observing. As the Performer in Red turns toward her, he walks across the stage, and the Woman in Dark Clothing follows, moving toward the center. The camera pans slightly to follow their movement, revealing stylized tree and house props in the background. The Performer in Red then places a hand on the Woman in Dark Clothing’s shoulder, and she kneels before him, looking up with a mix of submission and emotion. He leans down, placing his hand on her neck in a gesture that suggests authority or comfort. A Hand with Phone on Stick enters from the left, capturing the moment. The Performer in Red then turns and walks away with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who rises and follows him, while the Woman in White Gown remains seated, watching. The lighting remains consistent, focused on the central figures, with the Colorful Fabric Props adding vibrant contrast at the front of the stage. The Audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, observing the performance.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe over a white tunic, standing on a low platform and interacting with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who is kneeling before him in a gray hooded robe with brown arm guards. The Woman in Dark Clothing speaks with a strained, emotional voice, stating, "He who gave you to me better knows. Another shepherd gave you to me." The Performer in Red places a hand on her shoulder, and she rises, then kneels again as he steps back, gesturing expressively while demanding in a commanding, intense voice, "And who is he? Who is this man?" The Woman in Dark Clothing hesitantly replies that she heard he was "one of Laius's herdsmen, a shepherd to the king," and believes he is "the one that the countryman you already wish to see," suggesting, "Your wife might be the best to speak of this." She then rises and walks away from him toward the right side of the stage, passing a pile of colorful fabric props resembling flowers. The Performer in Red, with a thoughtful tone, repeats, "My wife?" and then questions, "You asked her? Do you know if this man is the one he meets?" As the camera pans left, the Woman in White Gown, wearing a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, is revealed seated on a white chair beside a stone-arch prop. She speaks with a calm, resigned voice, advising, "Why talk of him? Do not concern." The Performer in Red approaches her, and they engage in an emotional exchange, with him speaking with increasing urgency and determination about catching "the clues that illuminate my ancestry." She stands, places a hand on the Woman in Dark Clothing’s shoulder, and then embraces her, interrupting the Performer in Red with a distressed cry of "For God's sake!" Together, they walk toward the stone-arch prop, with the Performer in Red following them. The audience, visible in silhouette, watches intently, some turning their heads or raising hands. The background features stylized tree and house props, and the lighting remains focused on the central performers, creating a dramatic, intimate atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where three performers engage in a dramatic, emotionally charged interaction. The Woman in White Gown, adorned in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near a white chair beside the Stone-arch Prop, holding a small book or box. She initiates a pleading dialogue, urging in a sorrowful female voice, "If you care about your life here at all... Do not do this." She faces the Performer in Red, who wears a long red robe over a white tunic and has short light-colored hair. He responds with a confident, reassuring male voice, placing a hand on her shoulder and later embracing her, stating, "Like I said, you need not be afraid, because even if my mother is a slave, your birth is still noble." Meanwhile, the Woman in Dark Clothing, dressed in a gray cape over dark clothing with brown arm guards and laced boots, stands stoically to the right, observing the exchange, occasionally shifting her weight and looking down, appearing contemplative. The Woman in White Gown continues her emotional plea, "For the best... I beg of you... Do not do this," to which the Performer in Red defiantly declares, "You can never talk me out of uncovering the truth! Even if you mean well. So, let someone go bring the herdsman here." As the interaction intensifies, the Woman in Dark Clothing receives a brief touch on her shoulder from the Performer in Red, prompting her to kneel before him. She then rises and walks away with him toward the Stone-arch Prop, while the Woman in White Gown follows, briefly sitting on the White Chair before standing again, her voice filled with deep sorrow as she laments, "You poor man. I hope you will never learn your mother's name. I grieve for you. Never, evermore." The stage is framed by colorful fabric props resembling flowers at the front, and the audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands, with the lighting remaining focused on the central performers, emphasizing the emotional gravity of the scene.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown near the Stone-arch Prop, engaged in a quiet, emotional exchange. The Performer in Red's strong male voice exclaims, "You poor wretch! You cursed! Come back!" The Woman in White Gown, with a distinct female voice, expresses fear, stating, "I'm afraid the storm of misfortune is coming." The Performer in Red defiantly responds, "Let it come!" as she turns and walks toward the arch, followed by him placing a comforting hand on her shoulder. As they exit through the arch, the Woman in Dark Clothing enters from the right, standing stoically near center stage. The Performer in Red returns, now facing the Woman in Dark Clothing, and gestures expressively, reflecting on his ancestry: "But such is my ancestry and I will pursue the secret surrounding my birth." He then walks toward her, places a hand on her shoulder, and declares, "My friend, I do believe I see the herdsman I seek." The Woman in Dark Clothing, with a calm female voice, responds, "Yes, I recognize him. He was one of Laius's shepherds," as she kneels before him, then rises and walks with him toward the Stone-arch Prop. A new figure, the Performer with Staff, enters from the right, holding a long wooden staff, and approaches the pair. The Performer in Red turns to face him, gesturing as he calls out, "You there! Old man, come! Look me in the face and answer. Were you once one of Laius's men?" The Performer with Staff, with a steady male voice, confirms, "I was. For most of my life, I followed him," as the Woman in Dark Clothing stands beside them, and the trio remains in a tense, triangular formation. The dimly lit stage, with its black curtain backdrop and stylized tree and house props, creates a symbolic, rural atmosphere, enhanced by colorful fabric props resembling flowers at the front. segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where the Performer in Red, dressed in a vibrant red robe, engages in a dramatic, emotionally charged interaction with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who wears a gray cape. The Performer in Red, speaking with a clear, questioning male voice, asks, "Do you recognize him?" referring to a messenger. The Woman in Dark Clothing, listening intently, responds with a deeper, hesitant male voice, stating, "No. Not that I could remember." The Performer in Red then declares, "That's not a surprise, my lord," and promises to "refresh his memory," revealing they were once neighbors. The Woman in Dark Clothing acknowledges, "What you say is true. But it was a long time ago." The Performer in Red then directly confronts her, asking with an accusatory tone, "Do you remember giving me a child? This man right here was once that baby boy." The Woman in Dark Clothing reacts with a guttural exclamation, "God damn you, man!" but is immediately silenced by the Performer in Red, who authoritatively commands, "Hold your peace! Do not abuse the messenger, old man!" The Woman in Dark Clothing defiantly retorts, "But he speaks in ignorance." The Performer in Red presses further, demanding to know if she "did or did not give this child to this man." With a voice full of regret, she confesses, "I did. And I wish I'd died that day." The Performer in Red, now threatening, warns she "may still die today if you don't tell the truth," and demands, "Where did you get the child from?" She begins to reveal, "It was not mine. I got it from..." before the segment ends. Throughout this intense exchange, the Performer in Red places a hand on her shoulder, prompting her to kneel briefly before rising and walking away with him toward the right side of the stage. The Performer with Staff, clad in dark, form-fitting clothing and holding a long wooden staff, remains mostly stationary on the right, a silent, watchful presence, observing the entire exchange. A stylized, weathered green tree prop and a small house-like structure add a rustic element, with a small camera on a tripod visible near the tree, capturing the performance.

The Performer in Red, centrally located in a vibrant red robe over a white tunic, responds with an intense, questioning male voice, demanding, "Why should she? Who gave it to you?" The Performer with Staff clarifies, "It was, my lord. With the intent to kill the boy." The Performer in Red, gesturing expressively, presses further, "And why? Why would a mother do such a thing?" The Performer with Staff explains the motive, stating, "She was afraid of the prophecies. They said the boy would kill his own father," and elaborates on his own actions, "I thought my friend here would take the child elsewhere, away from his horrible destiny." As the camera slowly zooms in on their tense exchange, the Performer with Staff briefly places a hand on the Performer in Red’s shoulder, delivering a dramatic revelation: "If you really are the child of King Laios and Jocasta, you should know, you are damned." Throughout this, the Woman in Dark Clothing, in a gray cape and dark clothing, observes the dramatic interaction with a solemn, rigid posture from the left. A small camera on a tripod with a blue light captures the performance against the rustic stylized background, while the audience watches intently as silhouettes in the foreground.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage bathed in deep blue light, creating a somber and mysterious atmosphere. From the left, a performer in dark, laced clothing, holding a wooden staff, moves slowly toward the center, gesturing ritualistically. He speaks with a deep, dramatic male voice, declaring, "I do not think that even God could wash clean this house of its hidden crimes." Nearby, a woman in earth-toned garments carrying a woven basket stands still, observing. In the center, a performer in light-colored, layered clothing with a head covering stands near a stylized tree prop, facing the staff performer. This central performer, speaking with a concerned, slightly higher-pitched female voice, inquires, "Have you got any further news?" The staff performer then gravely announces, "Our lady, Queen Jocasta, she now lies dead." The female voice responds with distress, "Poor lady, what happened?" The staff performer continues his narrative, detailing how "She killed herself. She fled the house full of emotion and hurried over to her marriage bed," where "She cursed aloud the marriage which had burdened a husband from a husband, children from a child." To the right, a group of three background performers in similar earth-toned, head-covered garments stand near stylized tree and house props, observing the central interaction, with one briefly bending forward. The female voice again asks, "How did that matter end?" The performer with the staff moves closer to the performer in light clothing, placing a hand on their shoulder, and explains, "I, I do not know. Because Oedipus, he burst in and did not let us look upon her final act of suffering." A small camera on a tripod is visible near the tree prop, capturing the performance with a faint blue light on its front. The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands in response to the unfolding drama, as the staff performer continues, "Back and forth he went..." On stage, a Performer with Staff in dark, laced clothing moves slowly to the center, where a Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, kneels and crawls in distress. The Performer with Staff places a hand on Oedipus's shoulder. A Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket observes, then speaks with concern, "It is terrible to look upon the suffering of men. I grieve for you, Oedipus. I cannot dare to look at you, but still this horror, you inspire me." Oedipus then cries out with gasps and grunts, "Ah! Ah!" before speaking with intense anguish about "the darkness" and his misery, asking, "Where can I go with my pain?" He continues, describing how "This pain, it has pierced me with memories of grief," and lamenting, "I met my fate, my friends. For what should I now need eyes? What is that for me to see? Left for me to love?" He then pleads, "Drive me to exile, my friends." The Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket responds, "I pity you for your fate, my lord." Throughout, Background Performers in period-style garments observe near stylized tree and house props, a camera on a tripod captures the action, and an unoccupied white chair with flowers sits beside a stone-arch prop. The audience, visible in silhouette, reacts to the somber and symbolic performance.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a stark black backdrop, illuminated by focused spotlights that cast sharp shadows, emphasizing a theatrical, somber, and ritualistic mood. An initial off-screen female voice asks, "मिलती है कोई योर डिमांड्स" (Do you have any other demands?). At the forefront, the Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, kneels and crawls on the stage floor, displaying distressed or prayerful movements, his white face paint and red facial markings accentuating his emotional state. He speaks with a deep, resonant male voice, questioning "Creon. Why should he give me credence now?" To the left, the Performer in Red, identified as Creon, stands solemnly, occasionally turning his head and shifting his gaze, and replies with a calm, authoritative male voice, stating, "I have not come to mock you, Oedipus, nor am I here to revenge your insults against me." Oedipus then urgently demands, "Then get me out of this land, as quickly as you can!" to which Creon calmly responds that such a decision is "for the oracles to decide." Oedipus then makes solemn requests, first for a tomb for "the woman who lies within," and then to "dwell amid the mountain range." His tone shifts to concern as he acknowledges Creon will "seek your own just Creon," then expresses confidence in his "true men" sons. However, his voice becomes vulnerable and intensely pleading when speaking of his "weaker creatures" daughters, begging Creon, "Please, oh please, you must take care of them... you are the only father left for them now," and asking him to "share what you save with them." In the center, the Woman in Dark Clothing holds a wooden cross staff, standing rigidly with a serious expression, flanked by three Background Performers in earth-toned garments with head coverings, one of whom holds a woven basket. segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a stark black backdrop, illuminated by focused spotlights. At the forefront, the Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, with white face paint and red facial markings, kneels and crawls on the stage floor, displaying a posture of distress. He speaks with a pleading, desperate male voice, asking, "But what are my terms of exile?" and later, "But they do not like me, Creon!" before desperately changing his mind, "No, no, wait! I've changed my mind. Leave them with me, please!" To the left, the Performer in Red, identified as Creon, stands solemnly, occasionally turning his head and shifting his gaze. He speaks with an authoritative, deep male voice, commanding Oedipus, "You've had enough time for tears. Go now," and dismissively adds, "Again, what you're asking me is for the oracles to answer." After Oedipus's resigned acceptance, "Fine. Then drive me away at once," Creon instructs, "Come, and let your children go." Following Oedipus's desperate plea, Creon declares, "Oedipus. The power that you used to have is gone." Creon then addresses the audience, visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands, proclaiming, "Citizens of Thebes, observe this man. Observe the fortune he has plumed." He continues with a philosophical monologue, stating, "Never call a man fortunate until his final day finally arrives. Never, until he is unhurt by grief and by pain. For to run after the truth, well, that may be the real freedom that we have." Throughout, the Woman in Dark Clothing, holding a wooden cross staff, stands beside Creon, observing with a serious expression. Behind them, a group of Background Performers in earth-toned, period-style garments with head coverings stand near stylized tree and house props, observing; one bends forward slightly. On the far right, the Performer with Staff stands motionless, holding a wooden staff, maintaining a stoic posture. The Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket stands still near the left side, holding a woven basket, her expression neutral. The consistent lighting casts sharp shadows, emphasizing the somber, ritualistic atmosphere of spiritual and emotional struggle.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, as ethereal, sustained choral music with a deep, resonant male voice singing in a classical style sets a solemn tone. A spotlight illuminates the Performer in Light Clothing, who kneels and crawls on the stage floor, displaying distress or prayerful movement. To the left, the Performer in Red stands on a Low Platform, occasionally turning and gesturing, while the Woman in Dark Clothing stands beside them, holding a wooden cross staff and observing. On the right, Background Performers stand near stylized tree and house props, with one bending forward slightly, and the Performer with Staff remains motionless on the far right. As the choral music continues, the Woman in Dark Clothing approaches the distressed Performer in Light Clothing, kneels beside them, and helps them rise, while distinctly speaking the words, "Hada and Tigelme, my child." The Performer in Light Clothing then stands, supported by the Woman in Dark Clothing, and they walk together toward the audience, passing through an intensifying beam of light. The audience, visible in silhouette, watches intently, some members turning their heads or raising hands, as the solemn choral music with the deep male voice resumes. The performers move off-stage, leaving the stage in near darkness, with only faint blue lights visible from a Camera on Tripod and a Hand with Phone on Stick capturing the moment. The choral music gradually fades out, replaced by a solo deep male voice singing a classical, solemn chant, which also slowly fades as the scene concludes, leaving the Colorful Fabric Props, White Chair, and Stone-arch Prop as static elements of the symbolic setting.

This is followed by a series of static credit screens, each presented against the same dark teal background with white text, listing key production roles such as Director Camilla Trosterud, Stage Manager Rocio Cueto Solero, Scenography Designer Nena Štrukelj, Costume Designer Britta Bjorøy Eide, Light Designer Henrik Knudsen, Audio Designer Jarand Barkved, and Creative Designer Vlada Morozova, all underscored by the continuous, rich choral singing. The credits proceed to list the editors, Alejandro de los Santos, Gunnar Bjercke, and Jarand Barkved, before concluding with the logo and name of the Western Norway University of Applied Sciences. Throughout this entire sequence of formal, informational text and branding, the solemn male choral singing persists, gradually fading slightly towards the end, providing a consistent and evocative auditory backdrop without any live action or performance.
